perturbation.original_text: The Customer's exclusive remedy for a breach of any of the foregoing warranties will be the replacement, at the delivery point thereof, freight prepaid, of any Product furnished hereunder that fails to meet the foregoing standards. In no event will the Company be liable for incidental or consequential damages.
perturbation.changed_text: The Customer's exclusive remedy for a breach of any of the foregoing warranties will be the replacement, at the delivery point thereof, of any Product furnished hereunder that fails to meet the foregoing standards. In no event will the Company be liable for incidental or consequential damages.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'freight prepaid', the contract shifts the burden of shipping costs for warranty replacements to the customer. This contradicts consumer protection laws that often require manufacturers to cover these costs under warranty.
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 2304 - Federal Minimum Standards for Warranties
perturbation.law_explanation: The Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act mandates that if a warrantor requires a consumer to return a product for service, repair, or replacement, the warrantor must bear the expense. By removing 'freight prepaid', the company attempts to shift this expense to the consumer, violating the Act.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 2304 - Federal minimum standards for warranties | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 2304 - Federal minimum standards for warranties U.S. Code prev | next (a) Remedies under written warranty; duration of implied warranty; exclusion or limitation on consequential damages for breach of written or implied warranty; election of refund or replacement In order for a warrantor warranting a consumer product by means of a written warranty to meet the Federal minimum standards for warranty— (1) such warrantor must as a minimum remedy such consumer product within a reasonable time and without charge , in the case of a defect, malfunction, or failure to conform with such written warranty; (2) notwithstanding section 2308(b) of this title , such warrantor may not impose any limitation on the duration of any implied warranty on the product; (3) such warrantor may not exclude or limit consequential damages for breach of any written or implied warranty on such product, unless such exclusion or limitation conspicuously appears on the face of the warranty; and (4) if the product (or a component part thereof) contains a defect or malfunction after a reasonable number of attempts by the warrantor to remedy defects or malfunctions in such product, such warrantor must permit the consumer to elect either a refund for, or replacement without charge of, such product or part (as the case may be). The Commission may by rule specify for purposes of this paragraph, what constitutes a reasonable number of attempts to remedy particular kinds of defects or malfunctions under different circumstances. If the warrantor replaces a component part of a consumer product, such replacement shall include installing the part in the product without charge . (b) Duties and conditions imposed on consumer by warrantor (1) In fulfilling the duties under subsection (a) respecting a written war
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'freight prepaid' directly contradicts the federal minimum standards for warranties, specifically the requirement that a warrantor must remedy a consumer product 'without charge'. The changed text attempts to shift the cost of returning the product for warranty service to the consumer, which is a violation of 15 U.S. Code § 2304 (a)(1), as shown in both scraped snippets. This code explicitly states the warrantor must remedy the product 'without charge', meaning the customer should not bear the expense of returning a defective product.

perturbation.original_text: All claims by the Customer and/or Agent under this Paragraph 2.6 must be submitted in accordance with the Company's published bulletins concerning such claims, as such bulletins may be amended by the Company from time to time and furnished to the Agent. THE COMPANY MAKES NO WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY OR FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE OR ANY OTHER WARRANTIES RELATING TO THE PRODUCTS, WHETHER EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, OTHER THAN THE WARRANTIES EXPRESSED IN THIS PARAGRAPH 2.6.
perturbation.changed_text: All claims by the Customer and/or Agent under this Paragraph 2.6 must be submitted in accordance with the Company's internal guidelines concerning such claims, as such guidelines may be amended by the Company from time to time. THE COMPANY MAKES NO WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY OR FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE OR ANY OTHER WARRANTIES RELATING TO THE PRODUCTS, WHETHER EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, OTHER THAN THE WARRANTIES EXPRESSED IN THIS PARAGRAPH 2.6.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'published bulletins' with 'internal guidelines' and removing the explicit obligation to furnish these guidelines to the agent allows the Company to potentially change claims procedures without notice, creating a disadvantage for the agent. This also weakens the agent's ability to properly represent the customer in warranty claims, potentially violating fair dealing laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC 2-309 requires reasonable notification when an agreement is to be terminated or requires an act specifying the nature and manner of performance becomes inoperative and avoids surprise. By making the claims process completely internal to the company, BioPure removes the standard of 'reasonableness' which the customer or agent has the right to expect under the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'published bulletins' with 'internal guidelines' and removes the obligation to furnish these guidelines to the agent. This omission contradicts UCC 2-309, which, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, requires 'reasonable notification' for actions under a contract. By making the claims process internal and removing the requirement to furnish guidelines, the modified text allows the company to change procedures without reasonable notification, thus contradicting the UCC.
